examples.title: Algorithm: Access Matrix Model
examples.description: Demonstrates how the Access Matrix model works for protection in operating systems, showing domain-to-object access rights
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the Access Matrix Model, a fundamental concept in OS protection. It shows how domains (users/processes) are rows and objects (files, devices) are columns. Each cell contains the allowed operations. The algorithm includes functions to check access rights, retrieve Access Control Lists (ACLs) for objects, and generate Capability Lists for domains. The Access Matrix provides a theoretical framework, while ACLs and Capability Lists are practical implementations.

examples.title: Python: Access Control List Implementation
examples.description: Implements an ACL-based protection system showing how to manage permissions for users on different objects
examples.code:
"""
Access Control List (ACL) Implementation in Python
Demonstrates object-centric protection mechanism in operating systems
"""

from typing import Dict, List, Set
from enum import Enum

class Permission(Enum):
 READ = "read"
 WRITE = "write"
 EXECUTE = "execute"
 DELETE = "delete"
 PRINT = "print"
 MANAGE = "manage"


class ACL:
 """
 Access Control List - Object-centric protection
 Each object maintains a list of (domain/user, permissions)
 """
 def __init__(self, object_name: str):
 self.object_name = object_name
 self.permissions: Dict[str, Set[Permission]] = {}
 
 def grant_permission(self, domain: str, permission: Permission):
 """Grant a specific permission to a domain"""
 if domain not in self.permissions:
 self.permissions[domain] = set()
 self.permissions[domain].add(permission)
 print(f"[ACL] Granted {permission.value} on {self.object_name} to {domain}")
 
 def revoke_permission(self, domain: str, permission: Permission):
 """Revoke a specific permission from a domain"""
 if domain in self.permissions:
 self.permissions[domain].discard(permission)
 print(f"[ACL] Revoked {permission.value} on {self.object_name} from {domain}")
 
 def check_permission(self, domain: str, permission: Permission) -> bool:
 """Check if a domain has a specific permission"""
 if domain not in self.permissions:
 return False
 return permission in self.permissions[domain]
 
 def get_acl(self) -> Dict[str, List[str]]:
 """Return the full ACL for this object"""
 return {domain: [p.value for p in perms] 
 for domain, perms in self.permissions.items()}


class ProtectionSystem:
 """Simulates OS protection using ACLs"""
 def __init__(self):
 self.objects: Dict[str, ACL] = {}
 self.domains: Set[str] = set()
 
 def create_object(self, object_name: str):
 """Create a new protected object"""
 self.objects[object_name] = ACL(object_name)
 print(f"[System] Created object: {object_name}")
 
 def register_domain(self, domain: str):
 """Register a new protection domain (user/process)"""
 self.domains.add(domain)
 print(f"[System] Registered domain: {domain}")
 
 def access_request(self, domain: str, object_name: str, 
 permission: Permission) -> bool:
 """Process an access request - the core protection check"""
 # Principle of Least Privilege: only grant necessary access
 if object_name not in self.objects:
 print(f"[Access DENIED] Object '{object_name}' does not exist")
 return False
 
 if domain not in self.domains:
 print(f"[Access DENIED] Domain '{domain}' is not registered")
 return False
 
 has_permission = self.objects[object_name].check_permission(
 domain, permission)
 
 if has_permission:
 print(f"[Access GRANTED] {domain} can {permission.value} {object_name}")
 else:
 print(f"[Access DENIED] {domain} cannot {permission.value} {object_name}")
 
 return has_permission


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Create protection system
 system = ProtectionSystem()
 
 # Register domains (users/processes)
 system.register_domain("User_Alice")
 system.register_domain("User_Bob")
 system.register_domain("Kernel")
 
 print("\n--- Creating Protected Objects ---")
 system.create_object("Financial_Report.txt")
 system.create_object("Printer_Queue")
 system.create_object("System_Config")
 
 # Set up ACLs - Principle of Least Privilege
 print("\n--- Configuring ACLs (Least Privilege) ---")
 acl = system.objects["Financial_Report.txt"]
 acl.grant_permission("User_Alice", Permission.READ)
 acl.grant_permission("User_Alice", Permission.WRITE)
 acl.grant_permission("User_Bob", Permission.READ)
 acl.grant_permission("Kernel", Permission.READ)
 acl.grant_permission("Kernel", Permission.WRITE)
 acl.grant_permission("Kernel", Permission.DELETE)
 
 printer_acl = system.objects["Printer_Queue"]
 printer_acl.grant_permission("User_Alice", Permission.PRINT)
 printer_acl.grant_permission("User_Bob", Permission.PRINT)
 printer_acl.grant_permission("Kernel", Permission.MANAGE)
 
 # Demonstrate access requests
 print("\n--- Testing Access Requests ---")
 system.access_request("User_Alice", "Financial_Report.txt", Permission.READ)
 system.access_request("User_Alice", "Financial_Report.txt", Permission.DELETE)
 system.access_request("User_Bob", "Financial_Report.txt", Permission.WRITE)
 system.access_request("Kernel", "Financial_Report.txt", Permission.DELETE)
 
 # Display ACL for an object
 print("\n--- ACL for Financial_Report.txt ---")
 print(system.objects["Financial_Report.txt"].get_acl())
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates Access Control Lists (ACLs), which are an object-centric approach to OS protection. Each protected object (like a file or printer) maintains its own list of permissions. The code implements the Principle of Least Privilege by granting only necessary permissions to each user/domain. Key features include: permission granting/revoking, access verification, and ACL display. This is how file permissions work in systems like Linux and Windows.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for permission checks, O(n) for ACL retrieval where n is number of domains
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is total permission entries across all objects

examples.title: C: Capability List Implementation
examples.description: Implements a capability-based protection system demonstrating domain-centric access control
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates Capability Lists, a domain-centric (process-centric) protection mechanism. Unlike ACLs where each object maintains permissions, Capability Lists give each process/domain a list of capabilities (tokens) that grant access to objects. This mimics how file descriptors work in UNIX/Linux - when you call open(), you receive a capability (file descriptor) that grants specific access rights. The code implements: domain creation, capability granting with bitmask permissions, access verification by searching the capability list, and capability revocation (like close()). The Principle of Least Privilege is shown by granting minimal necessary permissions to each domain.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of capabilities in a domain's list
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is total capabilities across all domains
